sections.title: Ancient Iberia
sections.body: Long before Spain existed as a nation, the Iberian Peninsula was home to Iberian and Celtic peoples, with Phoenician and Greek traders establishing coastal colonies. Rome conquered the peninsula over two centuries, integrating it as Hispania and leaving behind roads, cities, law, and the Latin language that would evolve into Spanish. After Rome's decline, Germanic Visigoths established a kingdom that unified much of the peninsula under Christian rule by the 6th century.

sections.title: Al-Andalus and the Reconquista
sections.body: In 711, Muslim forces from North Africa invaded and rapidly conquered most of the peninsula, establishing al-Andalus, a sophisticated civilization renowned for science, philosophy, and architecture centered in cities like Córdoba and Granada. Over nearly 800 years, Christian kingdoms in the north gradually expanded southward in a prolonged process called the Reconquista. This culminated in 1492 when the Catholic Monarchs, Ferdinand and Isabella, captured Granada, completing the reconquest and unifying Castile and Aragon.

sections.title: The Golden Age and Empire
sections.body: The same year Granada fell, Columbus's voyage to the Americas launched Spain into becoming a global empire, bringing immense wealth from colonies in the New World. The 16th and 17th centuries marked Spain's Golden Age, producing masters like Cervantes, Velázquez, and El Greco, while Spanish Habsburg kings wielded unprecedented power across Europe. However, costly wars, inflation from American silver, and administrative overreach gradually weakened the empire's foundations.

sections.title: Decline and Turmoil
sections.body: The 18th and 19th centuries brought Bourbon reforms, Napoleonic invasion, and the loss of most American colonies through independence movements. Political instability persisted throughout the 19th century, marked by civil wars, shifting constitutions, and struggles between monarchists, liberals, and republicans. Spain's remaining overseas empire largely ended in 1898 when it lost Cuba, Puerto Rico, and the Philippines to the United States.

sections.title: Civil War and Dictatorship
sections.body: The Second Republic's social reforms in the 1930s provoked deep polarization, erupting into the brutal Spanish Civil War from 1936 to 1939. General Francisco Franco's Nationalist forces emerged victorious, establishing an authoritarian dictatorship that would last nearly four decades, suppressing political dissent and regional identities. Spain remained largely isolated internationally during this period, though it gradually opened economically starting in the 1960s.

sections.title: Democratic Transition and Modern Spain
sections.body: Following Franco's death in 1975, Spain underwent a remarkable peaceful transition to democracy, adopting a new constitution in 1978 and restoring constitutional monarchy under King Juan Carlos I. The country rapidly modernized, joining the European Economic Community in 1986 and adopting the euro in 2002. Today Spain is a parliamentary democracy grappling with regional autonomy movements, particularly in Catalonia, while remaining a vibrant member of the European Union with a diverse, service-based economy.
